subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference
- **Exactly 2 players** — Connect Four is a strictly 2-player game.

#### Core Components
- **Vertical grid frame** — 1 plastic frame with a 7-column by 6-row grid of slots.
- **Red discs** — 21 red checker-like discs.
- **Yellow discs** — 21 yellow checker-like discs.
- **Base/support legs** — Support structure to hold the grid upright.
- **Sliding bar** — Bottom release bar to empty the grid after a game.
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

#### Grid Specifications
- **Columns** — 7 columns, each holding up to 6 discs.
- **Rows** — 6 rows.
- **Total slots** — 42 positions (7 x 6).
- **Disc capacity** — 42 discs total (21 per player), enough to completely fill the board.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setup Sequence
1. **Assemble frame** — Attach the support legs to the grid frame so it stands upright.
2. **Close slider** — Ensure the bottom sliding bar is in the closed position so discs are held.
3. **Verify empty grid** — Make sure no discs are in the grid.
4. **Distribute discs** — Give one player all 21 red discs and the other all 21 yellow discs.
5. **Position grid** — Place the grid between the two players so both can reach the top to drop discs.

#### Table Layout
- **Grid between players** — The grid stands vertically between the two players.
- **Disc supplies** — Each player keeps their discs in front of them or in a pile to the side.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Player Assignment
- **Color choice** — Each player chooses a color (red or yellow).
- **No other roles** — Both players have identical roles and abilities.

#### Seating
- **Face each other** — Players sit or stand on opposite sides of the grid.
- **Equal access** — Both players must be able to drop discs into any of the 7 columns.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Pre-First-Turn Checks
- **Grid is empty** — No discs in any slot.
- **Slider closed** — Bottom bar is secured.
- **Colors assigned** — Each player has their color.

#### First Action
1. **Determine first player** — Youngest player, coin flip, or winner of the previous game goes first.
2. **Drop first disc** — First player drops one disc of their color into any column.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Flow
1. **Drop a disc** — On your turn, drop exactly one disc of your color into any column that is not full.
2. **Gravity** — The disc falls to the lowest available position in that column.
3. **Check for win** — After placing, check if you have 4 of your discs in a row.
4. **Pass turn** — If no win, the other player takes their turn.

#### Turn Rules
- **Mandatory play** — You must drop a disc on your turn; you cannot pass.
- **One disc per turn** — Exactly one disc per turn.
- **Column choice** — You may choose any of the 7 columns, provided it is not already full (6 discs).

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Placement Action
- **Column selection** — Choose one of the 7 columns.
- **Drop disc** — Release your disc at the top of the chosen column. It falls to the lowest unoccupied slot.
- **No removal** — Once placed, discs cannot be moved or removed.
- **No stacking on full columns** — If a column has 6 discs, it is full and cannot accept more.

#### Winning Connections
- **Horizontal** — 4 discs of the same color in a row along any row.
- **Vertical** — 4 discs of the same color stacked in a column.
- **Diagonal** — 4 discs of the same color in a diagonal line (ascending or descending).
- **Any direction counts** — A win can occur in any of these three directions.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Draw Condition
- **Board full** — If all 42 slots are filled and neither player has 4 in a row, the game is a draw.
- **Draw frequency** — Draws are uncommon but possible, especially between skilled players.

#### Solved Game
- **First player advantage** — Connect Four is a solved game. With perfect play, the first player can always force a win by starting in the center column.
- **Casual relevance** — This solved status rarely matters in casual play, as most players do not play optimally.

#### Pop Out Variant
- **Alternative mode** — Some editions include a "Pop Out" variant where players can remove one of their own discs from the bottom of a column instead of dropping one.
- **Pop Out effect** — All discs above the removed disc drop down one slot, potentially creating or breaking connections.

#### Grid Reset
- **Quick reset** — Slide the bottom bar to release all discs. Close the bar and start a new game.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Win Condition
- **Four in a row** — First player to get 4 of their discs in an unbroken line (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal) wins immediately.
- **Instant win** — The game ends the moment a player completes 4 in a row.

#### Draw
- **Full board** — If all 42 slots are occupied with no 4-in-a-row, the game is a draw.

#### Scoring Model
- **No points** — Connect Four has no point system.
- **Binary outcome** — Win, lose, or draw per game.
- **Match play** — Players often play best-of-3 or best-of-5 series.

#### Tiebreaker
- **Not applicable** — No tie is possible within a single game (it is either a win or a draw).

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### First Move
- **Center column** — The strongest opening move is to play in the center column (column 4). It provides the most connection opportunities in all directions.
- **Avoid edges** — Edge columns (columns 1 and 7) are the weakest opening moves due to limited connection paths.

#### Early Game Goals
- **Control the center** — Build presence in columns 3, 4, and 5 to maximize future connection opportunities.
- **Create threats** — Aim to build multiple partial connections (2 or 3 in a row) that force your opponent to respond.
- **Stacking strategy** — Build vertically in central columns to create diagonal threats.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Tunnel vision** — Focusing on completing your own 4-in-a-row while ignoring your opponent's developing threats.
- **Forgetting diagonals** — Most beginners miss diagonal connections (both their own opportunities and opponent threats).
- **Edge play** — Playing in edge columns too early limits your options significantly.
- **One-track attack** — Building only in one direction. Skilled opponents will block a single-direction strategy easily.
- **Giving away setup moves** — Placing a disc that inadvertently creates a stepping stone for your opponent's diagonal or horizontal connection.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Attack vs. Defend
- **Attack** — Build toward your own 4-in-a-row. Prioritize when you have multiple threats.
- **Defend** — Block your opponent's developing connections. Prioritize when they have 3 in a row with an open end.

#### Decision 2: Which Threat to Build
- **Double threat** — Create a position where you have two ways to win on the next move (a "fork"). The opponent can only block one.
- **Single threat** — Easier to set up but easily blocked.

#### Decision 3: Vertical vs. Horizontal
- **Vertical stacking** — Safe and predictable. Your opponent can see and block it.
- **Horizontal/diagonal** — Harder to spot and block, but requires more setup. Diagonal threats across central columns are the most powerful.
